entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for EEWills
notes: The file is 424,161 bytes at the recorded CME submodule commit.
Its DLPSTEXTCLASS structure contains 58 DIV1, 16 DIV2, 3 DIV3, 295 P, 54 L, 161 PB, 84 MILESTONE, 115 FOREIGN, 395 NOTE1, and 137 BIBL elements.

The header declares only Latin, but direct inspection identifies Middle English as the primary documentary language, Latin as a secondary documentary language, and modern English as the editorial language.

access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME digital text EEWills
access.format: HTML full text

rights.basis: The current U-M item labels the materials public domain and links the No Copyright–United States rights statement; the CME collection page says its texts are public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Current U-M CME electronic item and displayed text
notes: This is an attributed, current U.S. rights statement. It does not by itself decide copyright status in other jurisdictions or erase separately stated access terms attached to older exports.

entities.description: A manually keyed, page-oriented electronic representation of the 1964 EETS reprint, produced by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative and dated 1999.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text EEWills
notes: The XML editorial declaration names a keyboarding contractor rendered as 'Chrisian' and reports an error rate within 1 in 20,000 characters based on review of 5 percent of the text.
The encoding retains the edition title pages, contents, dedication, forewords, figure division, additions and corrections, 51 numbered will units, register-reference lists, and endnotes.
It explicitly omits the List of Names and Places and the List of Words and Subjects, printed on pp. 145–200, while retaining the edition's duplicated Arabic pagination for corrections and main text.

The 54 L elements occur in editorial framing and endnote material, including a Handlyng Synne quotation and a Latin antiphon with English translation; they do not make the testamentary collection a verse work.

rights.basis: The current U-M item labels the materials public domain and links the No Copyright–United States rights statement; the CME collection page says its texts are public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Current U-M CME electronic item and displayed text
notes: This is an attributed, current U.S. rights statement. It does not by itself decide copyright status in other jurisdictions or erase separately stated access terms attached to older exports.

rights.basis: The XML permits individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching with the statement retained, permits links, and expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
rights.component: Legacy 1999 electronic text distribution terms embedded in the XML
notes: The legacy availability notice conflicts in practical effect with the current public-domain/NoC-US presentation. Both claims are preserved rather than silently reconciling them.

bibliographic.citation: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454, copied and edited by Frederick J. Furnivall, Early English Text Society, Original Series 78 (London, New York, and Toronto: Oxford University Press, 1964; first published 1882).

bibliographic.title: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454

entities.description: The immediate printed source declared by the U-M electronic text: an OUP reprint of Furnivall's 1882 EETS edition, with reprint-specific imprint matter.
entities.label: Oxford University Press 1964 EETS reprint of Furnivall's Fifty Earliest English Wills
notes: The encoded verso states 'FIRST PUBLISHED 1882 / REPRINTED 1964' and identifies Vivian Ridler, Printer to the University, as printer of the reprint at Oxford.
No complete public scan of this exact 1964 manifestation was located during the dated search. The cached 1882 scan cannot establish all reprint-specific physical or typographic differences.

rights.basis: The 1964 manifestation inherits public-domain 1882 content but may contain reprint-specific matter or typography; no complete physical copy or explicit OUP rights statement was inspected.
rights.component: 1964 reprint-specific manifestation, including new imprint matter and any resetting or typographic contribution
notes: Do not transfer U-M's current U.S. public-domain statement to every manifestation-specific component or jurisdiction without further review.

bibliographic.citation: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454, copied and edited from the original registers in Somerset House by Frederick J. Furnivall, Early English Text Society, Original Series 78 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by Trübner & Co., 1882).
bibliographic.publisher: Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 78
bibliographic.title: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454

dateStatements.label: principal transcription work
dateStatements.notes: Furnivall's dedication describes copying during work begun in spring 1881; it is dated 4 December 1882, and the forewords are dated 5 December 1882.

entities.description: Furnivall's editorially selected and ordered compilation of 51 numbered English testamentary units copied chiefly in 1881 from registered wills in the Commissary Court of London and Prerogative Court of Canterbury registers, with dedication, forewords, corrections, notes, and indexes.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall's 1882 Fifty Earliest English Wills
notes: Furnivall's phrase 'original registers' means the probate register volumes he consulted, not necessarily the testators' signed originals. The National Archives identifies PROB 11 as registered copy wills.
The edition's title says 'Fifty' but the contents and text number 51 units because the 1454 will of Nicholas Sturgeon is added after the sequence dated through 1439.
Its 51 units include compound documentary presentations such as a will and codicil or testamentary supplements; edition numbering is not a physical-document count.

The main text occupies duplicated printed pp. 1–134 after four pages of Additions and Corrections; endnotes occupy pp. 135–143, followed by the List of Names and Places on pp. 145–169 and List of Words and Subjects on pp. 170–200.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1882, and Internet Archive marks the U.S. item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT; U-M also presents the inherited text as No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: 1882 edition text, editorial apparatus, and typography

workPortion.label: EEWills encoded portion: 1882 title and preliminary matter through endnotes on printed p. 143
notes: The complete cached volume additionally preserves the List of Names and Places on pp. 145–169 and List of Words and Subjects on pp. 170–200, both omitted from the XML.

notes: The OCR spans the volume but is an error-prone derivative and cannot represent page images or material features; use the PDF for visual evidence.

rights.basis: Internet Archive records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US; the PDF also contains Google provider notices governing use of the digitized copy.
rights.component: Google page images, Internet Archive PDF and OCR derivatives, and item metadata
notes: The provider's access notice and automated-query restrictions are not the same thing as copyright in Furnivall's 1882 text.

entities.description: A complete image-only PDF of an Oxford University library copy of the 1882 first edition, digitized by Google in 2007 and made available through Internet Archive in 2008, together with OCR and item metadata.
entities.label: Google/Internet Archive scan of Furnivall's 1882 volume
notes: Visual inspection confirmed the 1882 title page, complete editorial matter and numbered texts, endnotes through printed p. 143, both indexes on pp. 145–200, and the final printed page.
The scan contains no 1964 reprint title-page verso and is not evidence of the exact 1964 physical manifestation.

rights.basis: Internet Archive records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US; the PDF also contains Google provider notices governing use of the digitized copy.
rights.component: Google page images, Internet Archive PDF and OCR derivatives, and item metadata
notes: The provider's access notice and automated-query restrictions are not the same thing as copyright in Furnivall's 1882 text.

notes: The selected units are edition wills 10–15 and 18–21: Thomas Walwayn fol. 253, Richard Broke fol. 316v, John Solas fol. 337, John Chelmyswyk fol. 335v, Thomas Tvoky fol. 346v, Stephen Thomas fol. 358, John Bathe fol. 408, John Olney fol. 433, Peryne Clanbowe fol. 429v, and Roger Salwayn fol. 437v.

Known exact item records include PROB 11/2B/193 (Broke), /209 (Solas), /231 (Chelmyswyk), /403 (Bathe), and /424 (Olney). The edition's correction changes the Bathe testator's forename from Thomas to John.

next_steps: Inspect complete images for every selected TLA and TNA register entry, recording entry boundaries, endorsements, foliation, language, named testator, date, and relationships among will, testament, codicil, and probate act.
Search archive catalogs for surviving original wills separately; do not treat a PROB 11 registered copy or one edition number as an original or as exactly one physical document.
Reconcile edition corrections and current catalog headings, including John versus Thomas Bathe and Brown versus Broun.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact many-to-many crosswalk among Furnivall's 51 numbered units, individual wills, codicils or testaments, register entries, and any surviving original testamentary instruments?

next_steps: Inspect a complete physical or scanned 1964 copy, including covers, title leaf, verso, all preliminaries, pp. 1–200, and colophon.
Compare it page by page with the cached 1882 scan and the U-M milestones; request U-M production documentation or copy-specific metadata if available.
openQuestions.question: What manifestation-level changes, if any, distinguish OUP's 1964 reprint from the complete 1882 edition, and was U-M keyed directly from a particular physical 1964 copy?

next_steps: Ask The London Archives for microfilm reel identifiers, reel boundaries, completeness, and an assessment for digitizing the complete microfilm of each register; preserve the distinction between a microfilm surrogate and restricted originals.
Ask The National Archives whether complete-volume digital packages already exist for PROB 11/1, 11/2A, 11/2B, and 11/3 or commission condition-assessed reproductions through record copying.

Specify covers or bindings where available, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every folio recto and verso, attachments, labels, and all neighboring entries; identify the corpus work portions only after securing complete objects.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable digital surrogates be obtained for Courtney, Broun, More, Rous, Marche 2A, Marche 2B, and Luffenam, with every register surface rather than only Furnivall's passages?

next_steps: Request a written U-M clarification identifying which current rights statement controls bulk XML redistribution and whether the 1999 availability text has been superseded.
Request a repository-maintainer statement defining whether the Unlicense covers XML data and imported encoded texts, not just software.
Continue to preserve the 1882 edition, 1964 manifestation, electronic encoding, repository artifact, and archive-image rights as separate components.
openQuestions.question: How should reuse of the EEWills XML be governed when current U-M pages assert No Copyright–United States, the 1999 XML forbids redistribution by content providers, and the CME repository's Unlicense is introduced as a software licence?

reviewNotes: The primary cataloging subject is Furnivall's edition-level compilation, not a single medieval work and not the institutional author heading inherited from U-M.

The 51 numbered edition units do not necessarily equal 51 independent physical documents: several contain a will plus codicil, testament, or related instrument, and all surviving PCC sources identified here are registered copies rather than original signed wills.

The complete cached facsimile is the 1882 first edition. It must not be described as a scan of the 1964 reprint, whose reprint-specific title-page verso is visible only through the encoded source transcription.

Current U-M public-domain language, the XML's restrictive 1999 availability statement, Google access notices, repository software licensing, and archive-image terms are recorded as separate layer-specific claims.

lineage.summary: The repository XML descends from a University of Michigan electronic text dated 1999 and keyed from Oxford University Press's 1964 EETS reprint. That reprint reproduces Frederick J. Furnivall's 1882 editorial compilation of 51 numbered English testamentary units dated 1387–1439 and 1454. Furnivall copied registered wills and related instruments from three Commissary Court of London registers and four Prerogative Court of Canterbury register volumes; those registered copies are distinct from the original testamentary instruments, the edited compilation, both print manifestations, the Google/Internet Archive scan of an Oxford copy of the 1882 edition, the U-M encoding, and the checked-in XML artifact.

catalogSummary.author: Fifty-one named testators represented by registered testamentary copies
catalogSummary.date: Testamentary units 1387–1439 and 1454; edited selection first published 1882
catalogSummary.region: England, especially London and southern England; one selected codicil made at Rouen
catalogSummary.title: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London, A.D. 1387–1439; with a Priest's of 1454

catalogingSubject.label: The Fifty Earliest English Wills in the Court of Probate, London
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Frederick J. Furnivall's editorially created 1882 EETS selection and arrangement of 51 numbered English testamentary units dated 1387–1439 and 1454. The cataloging subject is not one medieval authored work, not one manuscript or probate register, and not necessarily 51 independent physical documents. Individual testators and testamentary instruments, seven aggregate registered-copy witnesses, the 1882 edition, OUP's 1964 reprint, the Google/Internet Archive scan, U-M's 1999 encoding, and the checked-in XML remain distinct descriptive and lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Title matter, contents, dedication, forewords, figure, and Additions and Corrections

locators.value: title matter; contents pp. v–vii; dedication pp. viii–x; forewords pp. xi–xv; Additions and Corrections pp. 1–4

notes: Verse quoted from Handlyng Synne in the forewords is editorial evidence and does not change the testamentary collection's prose form.
contentParts.summary: Edition framing that explains Furnivall's selection and transcription circumstances, surveys themes and language, acknowledges contributors and limitations, and supplies four separately paginated pages of corrections.

contentParts.label: Fifty-one numbered testamentary units
notes: Source distribution is Courtney 2, Broun 5, More 20, Rous 2, Marche 2A 1, Marche 2B 10, and Luffenam 11; these child counts total 51.
The numbering remains an edition structure. Complete register images are required before equating a number with one entry, one testamentary act, or one physical document.
contentParts.summary: Chronologically arranged registered testamentary texts dated 1387–1439 and 1454, including wills and some associated codicils or testamentary supplements from seven probate register units.

contentParts.label: References to other English wills in Luffenam and Rous
notes: These references do not enlarge the numbered collection's item count.
contentParts.summary: Edition lists or notes additional English-language register entries that Furnivall did not transcribe as numbered units in this volume.

contentParts.label: Editorial notes
notes: An embedded note contains a Latin Salve Regina and modern English translation encoded in verse lines; this is editorial quotation, not a testamentary unit.
contentParts.summary: Furnivall's and contributors' corrections, identifications, linguistic observations, historical commentary, quotations, and notice of the first known bequest of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales.

contentParts.label: List of Names and Places and List of Words and Subjects

locators.value: List of Names and Places pp. 145–169; List of Words and Subjects pp. 170–200

notes: This part belongs to the cataloged 1882 edition but not to the repository representation's encoded content.
contentParts.summary: The edition's two concluding indexes, preserved in the complete 1882 scan but deliberately omitted from the U-M electronic encoding and repository XML.

notes: The title's continuous 1387–1439 range is followed by an explicitly additional priest's will of 1454; it must not be normalized as an unbroken 1387–1454 run without qualification.

scope.description: Dated testamentary acts represented in the 51 numbered edition units
Furnivall's transcription, selection, arrangement, correction, and annotation of the compilation
First publication of Furnivall's EETS edited selection

extent.description: The cataloged testamentary collection contains 51 numbered prose edition units. The XML has 58 DIV1 containers: five front-matter divisions, 51 consecutive will-unit divisions, one register-reference division, and one endnotes division; it also has 16 DIV2 and 3 DIV3 containers. Raw XML counts include 54 L elements, but these occur in ancillary editorial quotations and a Latin antiphon with English translation in the notes, so they are excluded from the substantive will-form metrics. The XML's 51 units are not a physical-document count.

formStatements.description: A prose edited documentary collection of wills, testaments, codicils, probate language, and editorial apparatus. Verse excerpts in the forewords and endnotes are ancillary editorial quotations, not the substantive form of the testamentary units.

scope.description: Fifty-one numbered testamentary edition units, some including codicils or related instruments
Testamentary dispositions as preserved through ecclesiastical probate registration
Primary vernacular language of the selected testamentary texts

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML header's Latin-only language declaration is incomplete and is not propagated as whole-work language metadata.

scope.description: Testamentary and probate formulae, embedded clauses, citations, and an editorially quoted antiphon

languageStatements.label: English
notes: Modern editorial English is distinct from the Middle English documentary text.

scope.description: Furnivall's title, framing, headings, glosses, notes, translations, corrections, and indexes

notes: The XML's Church of England, Province of Canterbury, Prerogative Court institutional author heading is not used as the work author: 27 of the 51 selected units derive from the Commissary Court of London registers, and probate custody does not equal authorship.
The title's 'Fifty' is retained as title information; the edition and XML number 51 units because Nicholas Sturgeon's 1454 priest's will is appended after the 1387–1439 sequence.

Courtney, Broun, More, Rous, Marche 2A, Marche 2B, and Luffenam are aggregate registered-copy witnesses containing many unselected entries; complete-object requests must not be limited to Furnivall's folios.

Current TLA records direct users to microfilm and mark all three originals unavailable for general access; More is also unfit for consultation. The record does not represent the original registers as normally orderable onsite.
No public complete register facsimile or exact 1964 reprint scan was located as of 2026-07-14; the cached 1882 scan is complete for that edition and includes the two indexes omitted by U-M.

next_steps: Inspect complete source images for all selected register entries and record document boundaries, entry structure, language, named roles, foliation, endorsements, and relationships among instruments.
Search separately for surviving original wills; preserve registered copies, original instruments, and Furnivall's edition numbers as distinct identifiers and layers.
Build a many-to-many crosswalk rather than forcing 51 one-to-one document records.
openQuestions.question: How do the 51 numbered editorial units map to individual wills, testaments, codicils, probate acts, register entries, and any surviving original testamentary documents?

next_steps: Describe testator, declarant, scribe, registrar, probate official, witnesses, executors, and beneficiaries separately from complete source images.
Do not propagate the collective testator label, Middle English, London, or the U-M Prerogative Court author heading to every component without item evidence.
Distinguish original dialect from London register-copy spelling, following Furnivall's warning that copyists altered dialect.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact item-level creators, scribes, documentary roles, dates, languages, and dialectal features for each testamentary instrument?

next_steps: Inspect a complete physical or scanned 1964 copy and compare every page, including title leaf, verso, preliminaries, corrections, main text, notes, indexes, and colophon, with the cached 1882 scan and U-M milestones.
Do not cite the cached 1882 PDF as a manifestation-level facsimile of 1964.
openQuestions.question: What exact material or textual differences distinguish the 1964 OUP reprint from the 1882 first edition used to describe the corpus work?

next_steps: Obtain written clarification from U-M on whether the 1999 availability statement has been superseded for bulk XML reuse.
Obtain a repository-maintainer statement defining the Unlicense's scope for imported XML data and encoded texts.
Continue to separate text, edition, encoding, markup, scan, and archive-image rights.
openQuestions.question: Which terms control redistribution of the repository EEWills XML given current U-M NoC-US language, the restrictive embedded 1999 notice, and a repository Unlicense introduced as applying to software?

notes: London is an important archival and social center for the collection, not the place of origin of every selected testamentary act.

notes: The schema role author is used broadly for documentary creation. A registered copy may preserve a testator's act through a scribe or registrar rather than autograph composition.

notes: The edition's phrase 'original registers' identifies the register volumes Furnivall consulted; PROB 11 records are registered copies, not necessarily testators' signed originals.

scope.description: English wills and related register entries copied from the Commissary Court and PCC registered-copy volumes

notes: The XML omits both concluding indexes but retains the other identified framing and commentary.

scope.description: Dedication, forewords, corrections, headings, notes, and indexing apparatus

assertion.notes: The schema's collator role approximates several kinds of source assistance described by Furnivall; it does not imply a formal critical collation of every text.

scope.description: Source identification, assistance with difficult readings, and additional register-derived notes

subjects.label: Religious bequests and late medieval piety
Household goods, clothing, books, and material culture
Late medieval London and English social history

summaries.value: Furnivall's EETS volume assembles 51 numbered English testamentary units from the late fourteenth and early fifteenth centuries, chiefly wills dated 1387–1439 with an additional priest's will of 1454. Drawn from registered copies in three Commissary Court of London registers and four Prerogative Court of Canterbury register units, the texts document inheritance, landholding, household goods, clothing, books, religious bequests, burial, charity, occupations, family relations, and urban and regional networks. Furnivall framed the transcriptions with a dedication, polemical and linguistic forewords, corrections, source-register reference lists, extensive notes, and two indexes.

The 1882 edition contains title and imprint matter; a contents table; dedication to J. Challenor Smith; forewords; a figure division; four pages of Additions and Corrections; 51 consecutively numbered testamentary units on main-text pp. 1–134; lists of other English wills in the Luffenam and Rous registers; endnotes on pp. 135–143; a List of Names and Places on pp. 145–169; and a List of Words and Subjects on pp. 170–200. The U-M XML represents all stated material except the two concluding indexes and retains the duplicated Arabic pagination of corrections and main text.

This record describes Furnivall's 1882 edition-level edited selection. Its 51 numbers are editorial units, not a uniform count of original wills, registered entries, codicils, or physical documents. The source witnesses are seven aggregate probate register units whose many unselected entries remain outside the work. The U-M header's Church of England institutional author heading describes probate provenance and is not treated as authorship by the Prerogative Court, especially because 27 selected units derive from the Commissary Court of London registers. Item-level creators, scribes, documentary boundaries, and original-versus-registered-copy relationships require complete source-image inspection.

Drawn from registered copies in three Commissary Court of London registers and four Prerogative Court of Canterbury register units, the texts document inheritance, landholding, household goods, clothing, books, religious bequests, burial, charity, occupations, family relations, and urban and regional networks. Furnivall framed the transcriptions with a dedication, polemical and linguistic forewords, corrections, source-register reference lists, extensive notes, and two indexes.
